docs.title: Translation Status
docs.text:
This table tracks translation progress for the MkDocs site. English (docs/en/) is the source of truth. Nepali (docs/ne/) pages are created as stubs and translated progressively.

Page English Nepali Notes Home ✅ ✅ Home page stub translated Getting Started / Project Overview ✅ ✅ (stub) Needs full translation Guides / Contributors ✅ ✅ (stubs) submitting-data, using-forms, understanding-review stubs present Guides / Developers ✅ ⬜ Backend & auth pages will be prioritized later API / Reference ✅ ⬜ Auto-generated OpenAPI snapshot; examples may need translation

Maintainers: open PRs with docs/ne/ changes to add Nepali translations. Use this file to update status when pages are translated.

docs.title: HeritageGraph Documentation
docs.text:
Welcome to the HeritageGraph documentation site. This site collects guides and references for contributors, reviewers, developers, institutional partners, API users, and AI integrations.

  • Start with Getting Started → Overview
  • Developers: see Guides → Developers
  • API users: see API → Reference

If you don't find something, please open an issue in the repository.

docs.title: License
docs.text: HeritageGraph is released under the MIT License (or the project's designated license). See the repository LICENSE file for full text.

docs.title: Roadmap & Platform Plan
docs.text:
This page summarizes the phased roadmap for HeritageGraph (from PLATFORM_PLAN.md).

Phases:

  • Phase 1: Assertion-first architecture, multi-step forms, contributor onboarding
  • Phase 2: Record pages, triaged reviewer workspace, improved UX
  • Phase 3: Syncretic relationships, calendar conversions, mobile field surveys

For detailed milestones and implementation notes see PLATFORM_PLAN.md in the repository.

docs.title: AI Integrations
docs.text:
Guidance for integrating AI systems (MCP, agents, and other models) with HeritageGraph.

Topics to include: - Authentication and token flow for agent-based access - Safe query patterns and rate limits - Using the OpenAPI schema to generate client code

This section will expand as MCP integration details are finalized.

docs.title: AI Agents Guide (migration from AGENTS.md)
docs.text: This page provides a condensed orientation for AI agents and integrators working with HeritageGraph.

docs.title: What HeritageGraph is
docs.text:
• Full-stack platform to preserve and publish cultural heritage as linked open data.
  • Backend: Django REST Framework. Frontend: Next.js 15 dashboard, Next.js 14 landing.
  • Auth: NextAuth v4 with Google OAuth in production; Credentials/SimpleJWT in development.

docs.title: Key conventions for agents
docs.text:
• Read AGENTS.md in the repository root for the full agent-oriented instructions.
  • Avoid committing secrets; use environment variables from .env.example.
  • New features should use the CulturalEntity → Revision → Activity workflow not the legacy Submission.

docs.title: Useful endpoints (summary)
docs.text:
• Health: /health/, /health/detailed/, /health/ready/, /health/live/
  • API docs: /docs, /redoc/, /schema/
  • Heritage data: /data/submissions/, /data/cultural-entities/, /data/review/
  • CIDOC data: /cidoc/persons/, /cidoc/events/, etc.

docs.title: Where to look in the codebase
docs.text:
• Backend apps live in heritage_graph/apps/ (heritage_data, cidoc_data).
  • Frontend app code lives under heritage_graph_ui/src/app/.
  • Important files: AGENTS.md, CLAUDE.md, ARCHITECTURE.md, FORMS.md, AUTH.md.

This guide will be expanded and linked into the main MkDocs site. For the canonical agent instructions, read AGENTS.md in the repository root.

docs.title: API Overview
docs.text:
This section describes the backend REST API: authentication, endpoints, and how to use the OpenAPI reference.

  • Base URL: the configured API host (use NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL in frontend code)
  • Auth: Google ID tokens (production) or JWT (development)

See the full reference for endpoint details and example requests.

docs.title: API Reference
docs.text:
The complete OpenAPI snapshot is available as a static file and can be explored via the embedded Swagger UI.

  • Static schema: /api/static/openapi.json (committed snapshot)
  • Interactive: api/swagger.html page in this site

If you need a live schema, run the backend and use manage.py spectacular --file openapi.json.

docs.title: Production Deployment (summary)
docs.text:
This page summarizes the production deployment steps. For full details see DEPLOYMENT.md in the repository root.

Key points: - Traefik for TLS and routing - PostgreSQL as the DB - Use environment variables for secrets; do not commit .env - Health endpoints: /health/, /health/ready/, /health/live/

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text:
This section helps you get the project running and gives a high-level overview of the platform.

  • Quickstart: a minimal Docker-based start
  • Architecture: system diagram and data model overview

See the Quickstart for the fastest way to try the platform locally.

docs.title: Architecture — System Design & Data Flow
docs.text: This is a condensed migration of ARCHITECTURE.md summarizing system topology, data models, authentication flow, and frontend architecture.

docs.title: System components
docs.text:
• Traefik: reverse proxy and TLS termination
  • Backend: Django REST Framework (port 8000)
  • Frontend: Next.js 15 dashboard (port 3000)
  • Landing: Next.js 14 marketing site (port 3000)
  • PostgreSQL: primary data store

docs.title: Network & routing
docs.text:
• Docker networks: proxy (traefik), backend (internal)
  • Hostnames in development use *.localhost (frontend.localhost, backend.localhost)

docs.title: Authentication flow (summary)
docs.text:
• Production: NextAuth Google flow → browser receives Google ID token → API calls include Authorization: Bearer <id_token> → Django verifies token and auto-creates users.
  • Development: Credentials flow → POST /api/token/ returns SimpleJWT tokens → use Authorization: Bearer <access>.

docs.title: Data models
docs.text:
• Legacy: Submission model (flat fields) — used by older forms.
  • New: CulturalEntity → Revision (JSONField) → Activity workflow — preferred for new features.
  • CIDOC-CRM structured models in cidoc_data for person/location/event domains.

docs.title: Frontend app structure
docs.text:
• src/app/dashboard/ contains knowledge, contribute, curation, community, and graphview sections.
  • Use useSession() for client auth and getServerSession() on server components.

For the full original file with diagrams and detailed flow charts, see ARCHITECTURE.md in the repository root. This page is meant for quick orientation; deeper architectural decisions and diagrams remain in the canonical file until fully migrated.

docs.title: Architecture (summary)
docs.text:
HeritageGraph is a full-stack platform that includes:

  • A Django REST Framework backend (API, auth, data models)
  • A Next.js 15 frontend (dashboard, contribution forms, graph visualization)
  • A Next.js 14 landing page (marketing site)
  • Traefik as reverse proxy and TLS
  • PostgreSQL as primary database

See the repository ARCHITECTURE.md for full diagrams and deep-dive content. This page is a short summary for orientation.

docs.title: Project Overview
docs.text:
HeritageGraph preserves and publishes cultural heritage as linked open data. This page gives a short overview for newcomers.

Key goals: - Enable community contributions via web forms - Provide a moderation & epistemic review workflow - Publish data as linked open data aligned to CIDOC-CRM

See ARCHITECTURE.md, PLATFORM_PLAN.md, and AGENTS.md in the repository for the full vision and roadmap.

docs.title: Quickstart
docs.text:
Copy the environment template and start services with the Makefile (recommended):

cp .env.example .env
make setup
# or: docker-compose up --build

After services start, the important hosts are:

  • Frontend: http://frontend.localhost
  • Backend API: http://backend.localhost/api
  • API docs (Swagger): http://backend.localhost/docs

For local development see Guides → Developers → Local Setup.

docs.title: Contributors Guide
docs.text:
Welcome contributors — this section explains how to submit cultural heritage data, use contribution forms, and what happens during review.

Planned pages:

  • Submitting data: submitting-data.md (walkthrough)
  • Using forms: using-forms.md (field-level guidance)
  • Understanding review: understanding-review.md (what reviewers do)

For now, see the canonical FORMS.md and ONTOLOGY.md in the repository for detailed field and registry information. We will migrate and combine those into guides/developers/forms-system.md.

docs.title: Developers & Contributors
docs.text:
This section contains developer onboarding, coding conventions, and contribution workflow.

Planned pages:

  • Local setup and dev workflow
  • Backend (Django) conventions
  • Frontend (Next.js) conventions
  • Forms system and ontology registry
  • Testing and CI
  • Contributing guide (PRs, issues, branching)

See CLAUDE.md and AGENTS.md in the repository for agent-focused developer guidance; content will be migrated into this site progressively.

docs.title: Authentication Guide (Developers)
docs.text: This page summarizes how authentication works in HeritageGraph and provides quick patterns for frontend and backend developers.

docs.title: Two modes: Development vs Production
docs.text:
• Development: DevSessionAuthentication + SimpleJWT. Frontend uses a CredentialsProvider and POST /api/token/ to obtain {access, refresh}.
  • Production: GoogleTokenAuthentication. Frontend uses NextAuth with the Google provider; API calls send Authorization: Bearer <google_id_token>.

Detection is automatic: GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID env var present → production mode.

docs.title: Frontend patterns
docs.text:
• Client components: use useSession() to get session.accessToken and call backend with Authorization: Bearer ${session.accessToken}.
  • Server components / API routes: use getServerSession(authOptions) and forward the accessToken to the backend when making server-side requests.

Example client fetch:

const res = await fetch(`${process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL}/data/submissions/`, {
  headers: { Authorization: `Bearer ${session.accessToken}`, 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
});

docs.title: Backend verification
docs.text:
• In production, GoogleTokenAuthentication validates the Google ID token with google-auth and auto-creates/syncs a Django User + UserProfile.
  • In development, JWTs are verified via SimpleJWT.

docs.title: Quick checks
docs.text:
• Use INTERNAL_BACKEND_URL for server-side requests in Docker (http://backend:8000).
  • Ensure NEXTAUTH_SECRET is set for NextAuth.

For full reference, see the repository AUTH.md and the heritage_graph/apps/heritage_data/authentication.py implementation.

docs.title: Contributing (Developer Guide)
docs.text:
This guide replaces the minimal contributing.md at the repo root and describes how to contribute code.

Essentials:

  • Branch from v1 for all work.
  • Commit messages: imperative tense (e.g., Add feature).
  • Open issues for non-trivial changes and assign a clear description and acceptance criteria.
  • Use ruff format . and ruff check . before submitting PRs.

PR checklist:

  • [ ] Branch from v1
  • [ ] Include tests for backend changes
  • [ ] Run ruff format and npm run build for frontend changes
  • [ ] Update documentation in docs/ for any public API changes

See also CLAUDE.md and AGENTS.md for developer conventions and AI-agent guidance.

docs.title: Coding Conventions (Backend & Frontend)
docs.text: This page condenses the most important guidelines from CLAUDE.md and CONVENTIONS.md for contributors.

docs.title: Backend (Django)
docs.text:
• Python version: 3.13. Use ruff for formatting and linting.
  • New models: use UUID primary keys, created_at / updated_at, and explicit db_table.
  • Prefer CulturalEntity/Revision/Activity for new data models.
  • Use ModelViewSet and DRF @action for custom endpoints.

docs.title: Frontend (Next.js + TypeScript)
docs.text:
• Next.js 15, React 19. Use TypeScript for all new components.
  • Use named exports and props interface for components.
  • Use Tailwind CSS utilities; manage colors in globals.css via tweakcn.
  • Use use client only when necessary.

docs.title: API and Docs
docs.text:
• Use @extend_schema() to document critical endpoints for drf-spectacular.
  • Keep API examples and embed an OpenAPI snapshot under docs/static/openapi.json.

docs.title: Git & PRs
docs.text:
• Branch from v1. Use imperative commit messages. Include tests for backend changes.

This page will be expanded with examples and links to the full CLAUDE.md content.

docs.title: Forms System & Ontology (summary)
docs.text:
This page will merge the existing FORMS.md and ONTOLOGY.md into a single authoritative guide about the registry-driven form system, field types, enums, and migration patterns.

Current status: the canonical files FORMS.md and ONTOLOGY.md remain in the repository root. This page will be completed by consolidating those sources and removing duplication.

Key concepts to include: - Field registry and types - How to add a new entity type - Mapping LinkML/schema to frontend registry - CIDOC-CRM alignment and provenance via PROV-O

docs.title: Institutional Partners
docs.text:
Information for institutional partners: data sharing, licensing, metadata exports, and integration options.

If you're an institution wanting bulk exports or custom integrations, contact the project maintainers.

docs.title: Reviewers Guide
docs.text:
This guide introduces reviewer roles, the review workflow, and how to make review decisions.

See the review workspace and moderation guides for detailed instructions.

  • Review queues and triage
  • Decision workflow (community_reviewer, domain_expert, expert_curator)

More detailed reviewer tooling docs will be added here.

docs.title: Conventions
docs.text:
Project conventions (naming, branching, commit message style, and code layout).

  • Branch from v1 for feature work
  • Commit messages: imperative tense (e.g., "Add feature")
  • Do not commit secrets or large binary files

See CLAUDE.md and CONVENTIONS.md in the repository root for detailed rules.

docs.title: Forms System — How Forms Work in HeritageGraph
docs.text: Audience: Developers, AI agents, and contributors who need to add, modify, or understand forms in HeritageGraph.

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
1. Quick Start — Add a Field in 2 Minutes
  2. Architecture Overview
  3. The Ontology Registry (Single Source of Truth)
  4. How to Add a New Field to an Existing Form
  5. How to Add a New Enum (Dropdown Options)
  6. How to Add a New Form Section
  7. How to Add a Completely New Entity Type
  8. Field Type Reference
  9. OntologyClass Interface — Full Reference
  10. OntologyField Interface — Full Reference
  11. Custom Wizard Forms vs Auto-Generated Forms
  12. Backend Checklist — Django Model Sync
  13. File Map
  14. Troubleshooting
  15. Related Documentation

docs.title: 1. Quick Start — Add a Field in 2 Minutes
docs.text:
Want to add a "Commissioner" field to the Structure form? One edit, zero component changes:

File: heritage_graph_ui/src/lib/ontology/registry.ts

Find the architecturalStructure definition and add to the fields array:

{ 
  key: "commissioner",           // Must match Django model field name
  label: "Commissioned By",      // Human-readable label shown on form
  type: "text",                  // Input type (see Field Type Reference)
  section: "architecture",       // Which form section to group into
  order: 3,                      // Sort order within section
  placeholder: "e.g., King Pratap Malla",
  description: "Person or entity who commissioned the structure"
},

That's it. The form, data table, and detail view all update automatically.

⚠️ Backend sync required: You must also add commissioner = models.CharField(max_length=200, blank=True) to the Django model and run makemigrations + migrate. See Section 12.

docs.title: 2. Architecture Overview
docs.text:
HeritageGraph uses a registry-driven pattern. A single TypeScript file defines every entity type, its fields, dropdown options, and API endpoint. The UI components read from this registry and auto-generate forms, tables, and detail views.

                  ┌───────────────────────────────────────┐
                  │     src/lib/ontology/registry.ts      │
                  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────┐  │
                  │  │  OntologyClass definitions      │  │
                  │  │  (fields, sections, columns,    │  │
                  │  │   apiEndpoint, enums)            │  │
                  │  └──────────┬──────────────────────┘  │
                  └─────────────┼──────────────────────────┘
                                │
              ┌─────────────────┼─────────────────────┐
              │                 │                       │
              ▼                 ▼                       ▼
   ┌──────────────────┐ ┌─────────────────┐  ┌─────────────────────┐
   │  OntologyForm    │ │ OntologyData    │  │  Record Detail View │
   │  (Auto-generated │ │ Table (Auto-gen │  │  (Auto-generated    │
   │   contribute     │ │  knowledge      │  │   entity view with  │
   │   form)          │ │  listing)       │  │   sections)         │
   └────────┬─────────┘ └────────┬────────┘  └─────────────────────┘
            │                    │
            │   POST payload     │   GET list
            ▼                    ▼
   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │  Django REST Framework Backend          │
   │  models.py → serializers.py → views.py │
   │  (fields must match registry keys)      │
   └─────────────────────────────────────────┘

Key principle: The key values in the registry (e.g., construction_date, structure_type) must exactly match the Django model field names because OntologyForm sends { [field.key]: value } and DRF's ModelSerializer expects those names.

docs.title: 3. The Ontology Registry (Single Source of Truth)
Files
docs.text: File Purpose src/lib/ontology/types.ts TypeScript interfaces: OntologyField, OntologyColumn, OntologyClass src/lib/ontology/enums.ts Controlled vocabularies (dropdown options for select fields) src/lib/ontology/registry.ts THE registry — all 13 entity class definitions src/lib/ontology/index.ts Barrel re-export

docs.title: Currently Registered Classes
docs.text: Key Label Category API Endpoint person Person social /cidoc/persons/ location Place spatiotemporal /cidoc/locations/ event Event event /cidoc/events/ period Historical Period spatiotemporal /cidoc/historical_periods/ tradition Tradition conceptual /cidoc/traditions/ source Source provenance /cidoc/sources/ deity Deity conceptual /cidoc/deities/ guthi Guthi social /cidoc/guthis/ structure Architectural Structure tangible /cidoc/structures/ ritual Ritual Event event /cidoc/rituals/ festival Festival event /cidoc/festivals/ iconography Iconographic Object tangible /cidoc/iconographic_objects/ monument Monument tangible /cidoc/monuments/

docs.title: 4. How to Add a New Field to an Existing Form
Step 1 — Edit the registry (Frontend)
docs.text:
Open src/lib/ontology/registry.ts, find the entity definition, and add to its fields array:

// Example: adding "restoration_date" to architecturalStructure
const architecturalStructure: OntologyClass = {
  // ...existing properties...
  fields: [
    // ...existing fields...

    // ➕ ADD THIS:
    { 
      key: "restoration_date",       // Must match Django field name
      label: "Last Restoration",
      type: "text",
      section: "status",             // Groups into the "Status & Condition" section
      order: 3,                      // After "condition" (order: 2)
      placeholder: "e.g., 2015 CE, after 2015 earthquake",
      description: "Date of most recent restoration"
    },
  ],
};

docs.title: Step 2 — Add to columns (Optional)
docs.text:
If you want it visible in the knowledge data table, add to the columns array:

columns: [
  // ...existing columns...
  { key: "restoration_date", label: "Restored", sortable: true, visible: true },
],

docs.title: Step 3 — Add Django model field (Backend)
docs.text:
Open heritage_graph/apps/cidoc_data/models.py:

class ArchitecturalStructure(MetaData):
    # ...existing fields...
    restoration_date = models.CharField(max_length=100, blank=True)

docs.title: Step 4 — Run migrations
docs.text:
cd heritage_graph
python manage.py makemigrations cidoc_data
python manage.py migrate

docs.title: What auto-updates:
docs.text:
• ✅ Contribute form — field appears in the correct section
  • ✅ Knowledge data table — column shows if added to columns
  • ✅ Detail view page — field renders in the correct section
  • ✅ API serializer — included automatically (uses fields = '__all__')

docs.title: 5. How to Add a New Enum (Dropdown)
Step 1 — Define the enum in enums.ts
docs.text:
Open src/lib/ontology/enums.ts and add:

export const ontologyEnums = {
  // ...existing enums...

  // ➕ ADD THIS:
  CalendarSystemEnum: [
    { value: "Nepal_Sambat", label: "Nepal Sambat", description: "Nepal's indigenous calendar" },
    { value: "Bikram_Sambat", label: "Bikram Sambat", description: "Vikrama calendar used in Nepal" },
    { value: "CE", label: "Common Era (CE)", description: "Gregorian calendar" },
    { value: "Buddhist", label: "Buddhist Calendar" },
  ],
} as const;

docs.title: Step 2 — Use it in a field definition
docs.text:
{ 
  key: "calendar_system",
  label: "Calendar System",
  type: "select",                              // ← Must be "select" for dropdowns
  options: ontologyEnums.CalendarSystemEnum,    // ← Reference the enum
  section: "temporal",
  order: 4,
},

docs.title: Step 3 — Add Django model choices (Backend)
docs.text:
# In models.py
CALENDAR_SYSTEM_CHOICES = [
    ('Nepal_Sambat', 'Nepal Sambat'),
    ('Bikram_Sambat', 'Bikram Sambat'),
    ('CE', 'Common Era'),
    ('Buddhist', 'Buddhist Calendar'),
]

class YourModel(MetaData):
    calendar_system = models.CharField(
        max_length=30, 
        choices=CALENDAR_SYSTEM_CHOICES, 
        blank=True
    )

⚠️ Important: The value strings in the frontend enum must exactly match the first element of each Django choices tuple.

docs.title: 6. How to Add a New Form Section
docs.text: Sections group related fields under collapsible accordion headers. To add one:

docs.title: Edit the entity definition in registry.ts:
docs.text:
const architecturalStructure: OntologyClass = {
  // ...
  sections: [
    { key: "basic", label: "Basic Information" },
    { key: "architecture", label: "Architecture" },
    { key: "status", label: "Status & Condition" },
    { key: "location", label: "Location" },

    // ➕ ADD THIS:
    { key: "cultural", label: "Cultural Significance", description: "Associations with deities and rituals" },
  ],
  fields: [
    // ...existing fields...

    // ➕ Fields in the new section:
    { key: "primary_deity", label: "Primary Deity", type: "text", section: "cultural", order: 1 },
    { key: "ritual_significance", label: "Ritual Significance", type: "textarea", section: "cultural", order: 2 },
  ],
};

The OntologyForm renders each section as an AccordionItem. Sections with no fields are automatically hidden.

docs.title: 7. How to Add a Completely New Entity Type
docs.text: This is the most involved task. Here's the full checklist:

docs.title: Step 1 — Define the OntologyClass (Frontend)
docs.text:
Add to src/lib/ontology/registry.ts:

const inscription: OntologyClass = {
  key: "inscription",                              // URL-safe key (used in routes)
  label: "Inscription",                            // Singular label
  labelPlural: "Inscriptions",                     // Plural label
  description: "Ancient stone or copper inscriptions recording historical events",
  classUri: "crm:E34_Inscription",                 // CIDOC-CRM class (optional)
  icon: "file-text",                               // Lucide icon name
  apiEndpoint: "/cidoc/inscriptions/",             // Must match Django URL
  category: "tangible",                            // For sidebar grouping
  navigable: true,                                 
  sections: [
    { key: "basic", label: "Basic Information" },
    { key: "content", label: "Content & Language" },
    { key: "location", label: "Location" },
  ],
  fields: [
    nameField("Inscription Name"),
    descriptionField(),
    { key: "inscription_type", label: "Type", type: "select", section: "basic", order: 3, required: true, options: [
      { value: "Stone", label: "Stone Inscription" },
      { value: "Copper", label: "Copper Plate" },
      { value: "PalmLeaf", label: "Palm Leaf" },
    ]},
    { key: "language", label: "Language", type: "text", section: "content", order: 1, placeholder: "e.g., Sanskrit, Newari" },
    { key: "script", label: "Script", type: "text", section: "content", order: 2, placeholder: "e.g., Lichhavi, Ranjana" },
    { key: "date_text", label: "Date on Inscription", type: "text", section: "content", order: 3 },
    { key: "location_name", label: "Current Location", type: "text", section: "location", order: 1 },
    { key: "coordinates", label: "Coordinates", type: "coordinates", section: "location", order: 2, placeholder: "Lat, Long" },
    noteField(),
  ],
  columns: [
    { key: "name", label: "Name", sortable: true, visible: true },
    { key: "inscription_type", label: "Type", sortable: true, visible: true, format: "badge" },
    { key: "language", label: "Language", sortable: true, visible: true },
    { key: "location_name", label: "Location", sortable: true, visible: true },
  ],
};

docs.title: Step 2 — Register it in the export
docs.text:
In the same file, add to the ontologyClasses object:

export const ontologyClasses: Record<string, OntologyClass> = {
  // ...existing entries...
  inscription,     // ➕ ADD
};

docs.title: Step 3 — Create page stubs (Frontend)
docs.text:
Knowledge list page — src/app/dashboard/knowledge/inscription/page.tsx:

"use client";
import OntologyDataTable from "@/components/ontology-data-table";
import { getOntologyClass } from "@/lib/ontology";

export default function InscriptionKnowledgePage() {
  const cls = getOntologyClass("inscription")!;
  return <OntologyDataTable ontologyClass={cls} />;
}

Contribute form page — src/app/dashboard/contribute/inscription/page.tsx:

"use client";
import OntologyForm from "@/components/ontology-form";
import { getOntologyClass } from "@/lib/ontology";

export default function ContributeInscriptionPage() {
  const cls = getOntologyClass("inscription")!;
  return <OntologyForm ontologyClass={cls} />;
}

docs.title: Step 4 — Django model (Backend)
docs.text:
In heritage_graph/apps/cidoc_data/models.py:

class Inscription(MetaData):
    """Stone or copper plate inscription."""
    id = models.AutoField(primary_key=True)
    name = models.CharField(max_length=200)
    inscription_type = models.CharField(max_length=30)
    language = models.CharField(max_length=100, blank=True)
    script = models.CharField(max_length=100, blank=True)
    date_text = models.CharField(max_length=100, blank=True)
    location_name = models.CharField(max_length=200, blank=True)
    coordinates = models.CharField(max_length=50, blank=True, help_text="Lat, Long")
    note = models.TextField(blank=True)

    def __str__(self):
        return self.name

docs.title: Step 5 — Serializer (Backend)
docs.text:
In heritage_graph/apps/cidoc_data/serializers.py:

class InscriptionSerializer(serializers.ModelSerializer):
    class Meta:
        model = Inscription
        fields = '__all__'

docs.title: Step 6 — ViewSet (Backend)
docs.text:
In heritage_graph/apps/cidoc_data/views.py:

class InscriptionViewSet(viewsets.ModelViewSet):
    queryset = Inscription.objects.all()
    serializer_class = InscriptionSerializer

docs.title: 8. Field Type Reference
docs.text:
Field type values used in the registry and the expected UI widget:

Type Widget / Behavior text Single-line text input textarea Multi-line text area select Dropdown (single-select) — uses options or enum multiselect Multi-select control — options array required date Date input (string) coordinates Lat/Long pair input relation Entity search / relation picker (requires relationTo) boolean Checkbox file File upload (handled by DRF file fields)

docs.title: 9. OntologyClass Interface — Full Reference
docs.text:
See heritage_graph_ui/src/lib/ontology/types.ts for the TypeScript interface. Key properties:

  • key (string): registry key, must match backend model/endpoint
  • label (string): human label
  • labelPlural (string, optional)
  • apiEndpoint (string): REST API endpoint (must match backend)
  • sections (array): form sections
  • fields (array): OntologyField[]
  • columns (array): data table columns

docs.title: 10. OntologyField Interface — Full Reference
docs.text:
Key properties on each field definition:

  • key (string) — exact Django model field name
  • label (string) — human-readable
  • type (string) — see Field Type Reference
  • section (string) — the section key to place the field in
  • order (number) — sort order within the section
  • required (boolean) — show validation
  • options (array) — for select/multiselect
  • relationTo (string) — registry key of related entity

docs.title: 11. Custom Wizard Forms vs Auto-Generated Forms
docs.text: The registry supports both auto-generated forms and custom wizard-style flows. Use custom forms when the workflow requires multi-step validation, conditional field sets, or heavy client-side logic. Prefer auto-generated forms for simple CRUD-style data captured from contributors.

docs.title: 12. Backend Checklist — Django Model Sync
docs.text:
Whenever you change the registry keys, ensure the backend Django models/serializers match. Checklist:

  1. Add/rename model field in heritage_graph/apps/cidoc_data/models.py.
  2. Update serializer: either fields = '__all__' or explicit list.
  3. Run python manage.py makemigrations <app> and migrate.
  4. Add viewset and router entry in views.py / urls.py.
  5. If the field is used in filters, search, or ordering, update the viewset filterset_fields / search_fields / ordering_fields.

docs.title: 13. File Map
docs.text: Path Purpose heritage_graph_ui/src/lib/ontology/registry.ts Registry definitions (fields, sections, enums) heritage_graph_ui/src/lib/ontology/enums.ts Enum lists for dropdowns heritage_graph/apps/cidoc_data/models.py Django models that must match registry keys heritage_graph/apps/cidoc_data/serializers.py DRF serializers heritage_graph/apps/cidoc_data/views.py ViewSets and search endpoints

docs.title: 14. Troubleshooting
docs.text:
• Missing field in UI: confirm key exactly matches Django model field name.
  • Field appears but API rejects: run migrations and check serializer fields.
  • Enum mismatch: ensure frontend enum value strings match Django choices tuple first element.
  • Relation picker not returning choices: verify relationEndpoint and that the backend view exposes search parameters.

docs.title: 15. Related Documentation
docs.text:
• ontology/HeritageGraph.yaml — canonical LinkML schema
  • ontology/Heritage.ttl — OWL/Turtle export
  • FORMS.md (this file) is the canonical guide for the registry-driven forms system

docs.title: Ontology — HeritageGraph Ontology & Form System Guide
docs.text: Audience: Developers, researchers, and AI agents who need to understand, modify, or extend the ontology-driven form system in HeritageGraph.

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
1. Overview — How It All Connects
  2. Architecture Diagram
  3. Source of Truth Files
  4. Ontology → Frontend Mapping
  5. Registered Entity Types
  6. Controlled Vocabularies (Enums)
  7. How to Make Changes
  8. Field Type Reference
  9. OntologyClass Interface
  10. OntologyField Interface
  11. Ontology ↔ Backend Model Alignment
  12. Data Flow: Form → API → Database
  13. CIDOC-CRM Alignment Reference
  14. PROV-O Provenance Layer
  15. Troubleshooting & Common Mistakes
  16. File Map

docs.title: 1. Overview — How It All Connects
docs.text:
HeritageGraph uses a registry-driven architecture where a single TypeScript file (registry.ts) defines every entity type. The entire UI — forms, data tables, detail views, and navigation — auto-generates from this registry.

HeritageGraph.yaml (LinkML)        ← Canonical ontology (classes, slots, enums)
        │
        ▼
Heritage.ttl (OWL/Turtle)          ← Generated OWL for linked-data consumers
        │
        ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Frontend (heritage_graph_ui/src/lib/ontology/)                 │
│                                                                 │
│  types.ts     → TypeScript interfaces (OntologyField, etc.)     │
│  enums.ts     → Controlled vocabularies (dropdown options)      │
│  registry.ts  → Entity class definitions (fields, sections...)  │
│  index.ts     → Barrel re-export                                │
│                                                                 │
│  ↓ auto-generates ↓                                             │
│                                                                 │
│  OntologyForm     → Contribute forms                            │
│  OntologyDataTable → Knowledge tables                           │
│  Detail views     → Entity detail pages                         │
│  Contribute page  → Dashboard intent cards                      │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
        │
        ▼  (POST/GET via Bearer token)
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Backend (heritage_graph/apps/cidoc_data/)                      │
│                                                                 │
│  models.py       → Django models (must match field keys)        │
│  serializers.py  → DRF serializers                              │
│  views.py        → ViewSets + search                            │
│  urls.py         → API routes (must match apiEndpoint)          │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Golden rule: The field key in the registry must exactly match the Django model field name, and apiEndpoint must match the URL route registered in urls.py.

docs.title: 3. Source of Truth Files
docs.text: File Location Purpose Lines HeritageGraph.yaml ontology/HeritageGraph.yaml Canonical LinkML schema — classes, slots, enums, prefixes ~2060 Heritage.ttl ontology/Heritage.ttl Generated OWL/Turtle for linked-data consumers ~5190 types.ts heritage_graph_ui/src/lib/ontology/types.ts TypeScript interfaces (OntologyField, OntologyClass, etc.) ~103 enums.ts heritage_graph_ui/src/lib/ontology/enums.ts Controlled vocabularies for select fields ~230 registry.ts heritage_graph_ui/src/lib/ontology/registry.ts All 24 entity class definitions with fields, sections, columns ~900 index.ts heritage_graph_ui/src/lib/ontology/index.ts Barrel export ~17

docs.title: 4. Ontology → Frontend Mapping
docs.text:
When translating from HeritageGraph.yaml to the frontend registry, follow these rules:

YAML construct Frontend mapping Example classes: block OntologyClass in registry.ts slots: block OntologyField in each class's fields array enums: block Entry in ontologyEnums in enums.ts class_uri classUri property on OntologyClass is_a (inheritance) parentClass property on OntologyClass slot_usage (required, range) required, options, relationTo on OntologyField

docs.title: 6. Controlled Vocabularies (Enums)
docs.text:
All enums live in heritage_graph_ui/src/lib/ontology/enums.ts. Each is an array of { value, label, description? }.

Enum Key Values Used By Ontology Source ConditionTypeEnum Good, Damaged, Ruined, Restored Structure condition ConditionTypeEnum ExistenceStatusEnum Extant, PartiallyExtant, Destroyed, Lost, Hypothetical, Unknown Structure/Monument status ExistenceStatusEnum RitualTypeEnum 19 values (NityaPuja → ProcessionalMovement) Ritual type RitualTypeEnum DatePrecisionEnum Exact, Year, Decade, Century, Circa TimeSpan precision DatePrecisionEnum ArchitecturalStyleEnum Pagoda, Shikhara, Dome, Chaitya, Stupa Structure style ArchitecturalStyleEnum GuthiTypeEnum SiGuthi → RajGuthi (8 types) Guthi classification GuthiTypeEnum SyncreticTypeEnum Equivalence, Appropriation, Fusion, Historical Syncretic relationship type SyncreticTypeEnum LocationTypeEnum city, village, region, temple, monument, museum, archaeological_site Place type Custom SourceTypeEnum book, journal, archive, thesis, web, field_note, oral_history, inscription Source material type Custom TraditionCategoryEnum ritual, dance, storytelling, craft, music, festival Tradition category Custom EventTypeEnum festival, ritual, historical, ceremony Event classification Custom RecurrenceEnum annual, biennial, monthly, one_time Event frequency Custom ReligiousTraditionEnum Hindu, Buddhist, Syncretic, Jain, Animist, Other Deity/calendar tradition ReligiousTradition CustodianTypeEnum government, academic, community, museum, private, religious Data custodian type Custom DataCiteResourceTypeEnum Dataset, Text, Image, Audio, Interview, PhysicalObject, Collection Source resource type DataCite IdentifierTypeEnum DOI, ISBN, Handle, URL, LocalArchiveID, ISSN Persistent ID type DataCite VerificationMethodEnum cross_check, expert_review, field_visit, archival_comparison, oral_testimony Verification method Custom DocumentationMethodEnum 8 methods (photographic_survey → drone_survey) Documentation activity Custom FestivalTypeEnum ChariotFestival, MaskedDance, Jatra, Other Festival classification Ontology StructureTypeEnum 9 types (Temple → Other) Structure classification Ontology subclasses IconographicObjectTypeEnum Paubha, Murti, Other Iconographic object type Ontology subclasses MonumentTypeEnum Stupa, Chaitya, Other Monument type Ontology subclasses

docs.title: 7.1 Add a Field to an Existing Entity
docs.text:
Scenario: Add a "patron_deity" field to the Guthi form.

Step 1 — Edit registry.ts

Find the guthi definition and add to its fields array:

{
  key: "patron_deity",             // Must match Django model field name
  label: "Patron Deity",           // Human-readable label
  type: "relation",                // Field type (see Section 8)
  section: "function",             // Which section to place it in
  order: 2,                        // Sort order within section
  relationTo: "deity",             // Related ontology class key
  relationEndpoint: "/cidoc/deities/",  // API endpoint for search
  description: "Primary deity this Guthi serves"
},

Step 2 — Django model (if new field):

# heritage_graph/apps/cidoc_data/models.py
class Guthi(CIDOCBaseModel):
    # ... existing fields ...
    patron_deity = models.ForeignKey('Deity', null=True, blank=True, on_delete=models.SET_NULL)

Step 3 — Serializer + migration:

cd heritage_graph
python manage.py makemigrations cidoc_data
python manage.py migrate

Add the field to the serializer's Meta.fields list if needed.

That's it. The form, table, and detail view all update automatically.

docs.title: 7.2 Add a New Enum (Dropdown)
docs.text:
Scenario: Add a "MaterialTypeEnum" for construction materials.

Step 1 — Edit enums.ts:

Add inside the ontologyEnums object:

MaterialTypeEnum: [
  { value: "stone", label: "Stone", description: "Natural stone (brick, slate, marble)" },
  { value: "wood", label: "Wood", description: "Timber construction material" },
  { value: "brick", label: "Brick", description: "Fired clay brick" },
  { value: "metal", label: "Metal", description: "Bronze, copper, iron" },
  { value: "terracotta", label: "Terracotta", description: "Unglazed ceramic" },
],

Step 2 — Use it in a field:

{
  key: "primary_material",
  label: "Primary Material",
  type: "select",
  options: ontologyEnums.MaterialTypeEnum,
  section: "architecture",
  order: 4,
},

Step 3 — Django model:

Add corresponding choices tuple to the Django model field.

docs.title: 7.3 Add a New Entity Type
docs.text:
Scenario: Add an "Inscription" entity for stone/copper-plate inscriptions.

Step 1 — Define the class in registry.ts:

const inscription: OntologyClass = {
  key: "inscription",
  label: "Inscription",
  labelPlural: "Inscriptions",
  description: "Stone or copper-plate inscription with epigraphic content",
  classUri: "crm:E34_Inscription",
  icon: "file-text",                    // Lucide icon name
  apiEndpoint: "/cidoc/inscriptions/",  // Must match Django URL
  category: "tangible",
  navigable: true,
  sections: [
    { key: "basic", label: "Basic Information" },
    { key: "content", label: "Epigraphic Content" },
    { key: "location", label: "Location" },
  ],
  fields: [
    nameField("Inscription Name"),
    { key: "script", label: "Script", type: "text", section: "content", order: 1,
      placeholder: "e.g., Lichhavi Brahmi, Ranjana", description: "Writing system used" },
    { key: "language", label: "Language", type: "text", section: "content", order: 2,
      placeholder: "e.g., Sanskrit, Nepal Bhasa" },
    { key: "transcription", label: "Transcription", type: "textarea", section: "content", order: 3,
      description: "Full transcription of the inscription text" },
    { key: "date_earliest", label: "Date", type: "text", section: "basic", order: 2,
      placeholder: "e.g., 464 CE (Lichhavi)" },
    { key: "location_name", label: "Location", type: "text", section: "location", order: 1 },
    { key: "coordinates", label: "Coordinates", type: "coordinates", section: "location", order: 2 },
    noteField(),
  ],
  columns: [
    { key: "name", label: "Name", sortable: true, visible: true },
    { key: "script", label: "Script", sortable: true, visible: true, format: "badge" },
    { key: "language", label: "Language", sortable: true, visible: true },
    { key: "date_earliest", label: "Date", sortable: true, visible: true },
  ],
};

Step 2 — Register it:

Add to the ontologyClasses export.

Step 3 — Create the contribute page:

src/app/dashboard/contribute/inscription/page.tsx and knowledge list page stubs.

Step 4 — Django backend:

Add model, serializer, viewset, and router entry as usual.

docs.title: 8. Field Type Reference
docs.text:
Field type values used in the registry and the expected UI widget:

Type Widget / Behavior text Single-line text input textarea Multi-line text area select Dropdown (single-select) — uses options or enum multiselect Multi-select control — options array required date Date input (string) coordinates Lat/Long pair input relation Entity search / relation picker (requires relationTo) boolean Checkbox file File upload (handled by DRF file fields)

docs.title: 9. OntologyClass Interface
docs.text: See types.ts for interface definitions. Key properties include key, label, apiEndpoint, sections, fields, and columns.

docs.title: 10. OntologyField Interface
docs.text: Fields include key, label, type, section, order, required, options, and relationTo.

docs.title: 11. Ontology ↔ Backend Model Alignment
docs.text: Golden rule repeated: registry keys must match Django field names exactly; apiEndpoint must match urls.py route.

docs.title: 12. Data Flow: Form → API → Database
docs.text: User fills OntologyForm → submit JSON payload matching key names → DRF ModelSerializer → Django model saved.

docs.title: 13. CIDOC-CRM Alignment Reference
docs.text: The project maps key classes and properties to CIDOC-CRM URIs where appropriate; see mapping table in the main ontology files.

docs.title: 14. PROV-O Provenance Layer
docs.text: We add provenance layering to store documentation activities and verification steps aligned to PROV-O where required.

docs.title: 15. Troubleshooting & Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Field name mismatches between frontend and Django cause 400s; check names and run migrations.
  • Duplicate component names in OpenAPI can appear when multiple serializers expose similar models; add explicit component_name annotations if needed.
  • Missing serializer_class on APIView subclasses will cause drf-spectacular to guess and produce weak schemas.

docs.title: 16. File Map
docs.text:
Files and approximate locations are repeated here for convenience.

For full canonical schema, see ontology/HeritageGraph.yaml and the exported ontology/Heritage.ttl.

docs.title: Skills Matrix
docs.text: This page will mirror SKILLS.md and track feature status across the platform (working/partial/planned). For now consult SKILLS.md in the repository for the authoritative table.

docs.title: Translation & i18n Workflow
docs.text:
We use mkdocs-static-i18n to provide English (en) and Nepali (ne) content.

Workflow:

  1. English is the source of truth; write new content under docs/en/.
  2. Create corresponding Nepali stubs under docs/ne/ with [Translation needed] markers.
  3. Translators update docs/ne/* pages; pull requests should include both en and ne changes where available.
  4. CI builds the MkDocs site and publishes to GitHub Pages. The language switcher appears in the site header.

Priority pages for translation:

  • index.md (home)
  • guides/contributors/*
  • getting-started/project-overview.md
  • about/roadmap.md

Translation status can be tracked in docs/translation-status.md (TODO: add table).

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
This page is a mirror of TROUBLESHOOTING.md in the repository root. It will be expanded with actionable debugging steps and links to logs.

Common issues: - Duplicate NextAuth config - Hardcoded backend URLs in README - Docker compose quirks — use make setup to reproduce the recommended workflow

See TROUBLESHOOTING.md for the full list of known issues and fixes.

docs.title: HeritageGraph Documentation (नेपाली)
docs.text:
Welcome — this Nepali section is under active translation. For full English docs see the main site.

Original English content:

# HeritageGraph Documentation

Welcome to the HeritageGraph documentation site. This site collects guides and references for contributors, reviewers, developers, institutional partners, API users, and AI integrations.

- Start with Getting Started → Overview
- Developers: see Guides → Developers
- API users: see API → Reference

If you don't find something, please open an issue in the repository.

यो साइटले योगदानकर्ता, विकासकर्ता र सहभागीहरूको लागि आधारभूत दस्तावेजहरू समेट्नेछ।

यो अहिले प्रगतिको अवस्थामा छ — धेरै पृष्ठहरू अंग्रेजीमा उपलब्ध छन्।

docs.title: रोडम्याप (नेपाली)
docs.text:
This page is a Nepali placeholder copied from the English roadmap; please translate when ready.

Roadmap and major phases (Phase 1, Phase 2, Phase 3) will be summarized here.

docs.title: परियोजना अवलोकन (नेपाली)
docs.text:
This is a Nepali placeholder containing the English project overview for translation.

# Project Overview

HeritageGraph preserves and publishes cultural heritage as linked open data. This page gives a short overview for newcomers.

Key goals:
- Enable community contributions via web forms
- Provide a moderation & epistemic review workflow
- Publish data as linked open data aligned to CIDOC-CRM

See `ARCHITECTURE.md`, `PLATFORM_PLAN.md`, and `AGENTS.md` in the repository for the full vision and roadmap.

यो पृष्ठ अझै विकासको अवस्थामा छ — पूर्ण सामग्री अंग्रेजीमा docs/en/guides/contributors/ मा छ र क्रमशः अनुवाद गरिनेछ।

docs.text: [Translation needed] यो पृष्ठले प्रत्येक फिल्डको अर्थ, अनिवार्य फिल्डहरू, र कसरी सही रूपमा जानकारी प्रविष्ट गर्ने भनी व्याख्या गर्नेछ।
